steps.text: Use an independent FTIR and sampling system to measure the native source concentration throughout each standard addition.
Perform pre and post-test spiking consisting of at least 3 replicates with the following measurement sequence: native gas concentration, SA-elevated gas concentration, native gas concentration.
Ensure that spiking is performed after each run to ensure continued compliance with the required spike recovery criteria.
If spiking is not performed after each run and the post-test spike fails, invalidate all data for that test.
If spiking is performed after each run, use data bracketed on each end by a successful spike as valid test data.
Ensure that your spike gas flow rate does not contribute more than 10% of the total volumetric flow rate through the FTIR.
Determine the response time (RT) of the system by injecting zero air into the system, then measuring the native stack concentration of the species to be spiked.
Confirm that the concentration has stabilized when variability appears constant for five minutes.
Determine a dilution factor (DF) for each dynamic spike via a tracer.
Use the equation for dilution factor calculation where the tracer is not native to the source emissions.
Use the equation for dilution factor calculation where the tracer gas is native to the source emissions.

Calculate the standard addition response (SAR) as the difference between the measured native source concentration and the concentration measured upon introduction of the standard addition via dynamic spike.
Use section 5.3 of Method 301 (40 CFR part 63, appendix A), the Analyte Spike procedure, with these modifications.
The analyte spike is performed dynamically. This means the spike flow is continuous and constant as spiked samples are measured.
Introduce the spike gas at the back of the sample probe.
Spiked effluent is carried through all sampling components downstream of the probe.
A single FTIR system (or more) may be used to collect and analyze spectra (not quadruplicate integrated sampling trains).
All of the validation measurements are performed sequentially in a single 'run' (section 3.23 of this method).
The measurements analyzed statistically are each independent (section 3.22 of this method).
A validation data set must consist of 12 or more spike replicates.
Collect two background spectra in succession while the nitrogen or zero air is continuously flowing through the cell.
Calculate NEARMSCTS per the provided equation.
Obtain reference spectra for each analyte, interferant, surrogate, CTS, and tracer.
Report traceability and other pertinent information for each reference spectrum.
Ensure that resolution, line position, and apodization function used for collection of sample spectra are the same as those of the reference spectra used for quantitation.
Ensure reference spectra for each target compound bracket the concentration of that compound in the sample stream.
Seal the probe end of the system by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Start sampling pumps and operate them until the pressure stabilizes.
Observe/measure the flow through the vacuum-side of the sampling system. A flow of less than 0.5% of the system’s normal in-use flow rate is acceptable.
Seal the probe end of the system as close to the probe opening as possible by capping or plugging the end of the sample probe.
Operate all vacuum pumps.
Draw a vacuum on the sampling system and let the pressure on the system stabilize.
Turn off the sample pumps and seal the system under a vacuum of 250 mmHg greater than the source static pressure.
Record the absolute pressure and the system absolute temperature every 30 seconds for 5 minutes. The leak rate must be equal to or less than 2.5 mmHg per minute.
If the spike recovery, as calculated by Equation 5 of this method, is within 70–130%, then proceed with the testing.
Use a non-certified cylinder for an analyte that has been validated at the source.
If the spike recovery, as calculated by equation 5 of this method, is within 90–110%, then proceed with the testing.
Sampling must be done using a continuous flow of source gas.
A stratification check must be performed, per the steps in this section, to justify sampling at a single location during testing.
Use a probe of appropriate length to measure the analyte of interest at each of 12 traverse points (MNi, where i = 1 to 12).
Calculate the mean measured concentration for all sampling points (MNavg).
Calculate the percent stratification (St) of each traverse point using the following equation.

constraints.text: Surrogates are not allowed for the following analytes: formaldehyde, HCl, HF, NH3, and vinyl chloride.
